text: The surgeon performs laparoscopic dissection of the right hemicolon. The omentum is stressed over the second part of the duodenum, and a gauze piece is used to create space, revealing the duodenum clearly. The mesentery is held upward while the duodenum is pushed downward, creating a retroperitoneal space without energy sources. The third part of the duodenum is exposed, and fat is removed from above the vessel, making the right colic artery bare. Clips are placed proximally and distally on the artery.

The surgeon continues dissection towards the lateral abdominal wall, revealing the gonadal vessels and ureter. The ureter is positioned medial to the gonadal vessels, which are kept anterior during dissection. The colon is pushed upwards to facilitate exposure, and the ureter and gonadal vessels are seen turning downwards as dissection progresses.

The surgeon completes the medial to lateral dissection by dividing the vessel, then begins the lateral to medial dissection. The appendix and cecum are held with medial traction while a nick is made in the lateral abdominal wall fold using a harmonic scalpel. Carbon dioxide insufflation aids in maintaining a clear field, and continuous traction over the cecum facilitates dissection towards the hepatic flexure of the colon, pushing the colon medially and downwards.

The surgeon visualizes the liver and gallbladder before proceeding with the dissection of the transverse colon. The transverse colon is separated from the mesocolon by applying upward traction on the mesocolon and pushing the colon downwards. The transverse mesocolon remnant is completely divided. The right branch of the middle colic vein is clipped and divided using harmonic, followed by the separation and clipping of the right branch of the middle colic artery, with two clips proximally and one distally, before division. The specimen is removed, and the anastomosis between the transverse colon and ileum is performed extracorporeally.
